metadata.title: Why the Born Rule Is Just Measuring the Length of a Shadow
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = eigenstate directions / definite outcomes; CRIMSON = amplitude (complex, before squaring); GOLD = probability squares (highlight fill only, never text)
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no polarization-identity proof, no density matrix, no no-go theorems, no collapse postulate debate. Mechanism only: squared projection length as geometric probability.

beats.narration_text: The Born rule — the formula that turns quantum amplitudes into probabilities — looks like a new postulate bolted onto the theory. It isn't. It describes a geometric measurement you could draw with a ruler.

card.copy: Why the Born Rule Is Just Measuring the Length of a Shadow

beats.narration_text: Take a spin-x eigenstate — a state pointing along x in Hilbert space. Ask whether it will measure spin-up. The Born rule says: square the inner product. The answer is one half.
Draw it: the z-eigenstate points up. The x-eigenstate sits at forty-five degrees. The shadow of x onto z has length one over root two. Squaring that gives one half — the probability.

The Question: the Born rule says probability equals the squared inner product. If the inner product is the shadow, why squared? And why does that give something that actually behaves like a probability?

card.copy: P = |⟨a|ψ⟩|²  →  squared projection. Why squared, and why does that sum to 1?

beats.narration_text: Naive answer: just use the inner product directly — not squared. But the inner product is a complex number. A probability must be real and non-negative. The square of the modulus is the only operation that achieves both.

The mechanism: expand the state in the eigenbasis. Each coefficient is a complex amplitude — a projection. The squared modulus of that coefficient is the squared shadow length. That is real, non-negative, and — because the state is normalized — all the squared shadows sum to exactly one.

Draw a unit-length state vector in a two-dimensional Hilbert space. Project it onto the first eigenstate: shadow length c₁. Project onto the second: shadow length c₂. The squared lengths — the probability squares — tile the unit circle and add to one.

The phase disappears when you square. The amplitude c₁ equals its modulus times e to the i-theta. Square the modulus and the phase is gone — it survives only in interference, when two amplitudes feed the same outcome.

The implication: the Born rule is not a separate axiom. It is the only way to extract a real, non-negative, unit-sum number from a complex-amplitude decomposition in a Hilbert space. The geometry forces it.

A student prepares a state at thirty degrees from the x-eigenstate, in a two-level system. She computes: P of E-one equals cosine-squared thirty — about 0.75. P of E-two equals sine-squared thirty — 0.25. She prepares one thousand copies and measures: roughly 750 land in E-one, 250 in E-two. The shadow lengths predicted the histogram.
beats.example_note: illustrative — numbers from the card's example seed

beats.narration_text: Why does the Born rule square the inner product? Because the inner product is a complex amplitude — a shadow in Hilbert space. To make a probability, you must square the modulus. And because the state has unit length, all those squared shadows add to exactly one.

card.copy: P = |⟨a|ψ⟩|²  =  squared shadow length.  Sums to 1 because |ψ⟩ is a unit vector.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom series.
